dialogue.utterance: Unit 7 responding for overdose near RRHA apartments. Patient is unconscious.
Hi, I'm Taylor, an EMT with the city squad. What happened when you found him today?
We found him lying in the grass barely breathing. I think he took something-maybe an overdose.
Is he unconscious or showing any signs of awareness?
No, his eyes stayed closed the whole time.
Does he respond to verbal commands? Can he speak?
No movement, just lying there very still.
Let's start with a nasopharyngeal airway. He's not breathing effectively on his own.
Respirations are shallow and at 8 per minute. Spo2 is very low at 53% in room air.
Pulse is 105, and blood pressure is 123/78.
SpO2 is at 53. We need high-flow oxygen right away.
Starting oxygen via BVM at 15 liters per minute for immediate support.
Nasopharyngeal airway successfully placed. Airway is now patent and secured.
GCS is 3-unresponsive to all stimuli with no eye or motor responses.
Skin is diaphoretic, no signs of trauma or external injuries.
Pupils are pinpoint and non-reactive. Breathing is shallow and sonorous.
Let's place him on 4-lead EKG monitoring. I'll set it up now.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia with a heart rate of 105.
Airway is stable with NPA in place, but breathing remains shallow.
Preparing for IV access in the left arm at a distal site with an 18-gauge catheter using aseptic techniques.
Administering first dose of Naloxone 0.25 mg IV for opioid reversal.
SpO2 increased to 100 after Naloxone. Pulse is 111, BP is 108/64, and respirations are at 12.
Glucose is at 0 now. It will be monitored throughout transport.
Capnography initiated for continuous CO2 monitoring.
EKG monitoring ongoing with stable sinus rhythm noted.
Administering second Naloxone 0.25 mg IV now.
BP is 109/63. SpO2 is maintaining at 100, and respirations are at 12.
Does he have any known medical history of substance use or mental illness?
Yes, history includes opioid abuse, use of sedative-hypnotic medicines, and major depression, bipolar, and schizoaffective disorder.
Any allergies or medications he takes on a regular basis?
No known allergies reported. No medications found on him or reported by others today.
When was the last time you think he ate or drank before this event?
Around the time he was found. Not sure of the exact time before symptoms started.
He's not reporting any pain-he's unresponsive, so pain score is 0.
We'll check his airway again, as he might pull out the NPA later while restless or confused.
SpO2 dropped slightly to 90. Adjusting to nasal cannula at 4 LPM now for comfort and security.
Respirations improved to 16 per minute. Breathing is better now but needs close monitoring.
Pulse is down to 108. Still above normal but stable at the moment.
SpO2 briefly dropped to 90. Oxygen adjusted to 4 LNC for continuous support.
Administering third Naloxone of 0.5 mg IV due to late response and to maintain effect while transporting.
SpO2 returned to 96 within 20 minutes. Respirations remain steady at 16 per minute.
Known history includes opioid abuse, sedative/hypnotic abuse, and mental health conditions like bipolar and schizoaffective disorder.
He briefly became aggressive and pulled out the NPA, which had visible blood clots, but he did not vomit anything.
He attempted to vomit after pulling out the NPA but was not able to produce anything.
Signs indicate possible recurrent or delayed drug effects. He briefly became aware before lapsing back into a sleep-like state again.
Let's move him carefully. He's stable for transport in a semi-Fowler's position with support for airway and oxygen delivery maintained.
Transport destination is VCU ED for full evaluation and treatment given his overdose and complex medical history.
Positioned in semi-Fowler's with oxygen on via nasal cannula. All straps are tight and securing for transport safety.
Patient found unconscious in grass, barely breathing with a GCS of 3 and shallow resps.
Vital signs improved with Naloxone and BVM support-initial 123/78, later 114/71 with no hypoxia sustained during treatment.
IV line in place with saline lock, three Naloxone doses administered-0.25, 0.25, and 0.5 mg-timed at intervals for maximum effect.
Capnography and EKG used for ongoing monitoring of vital signs and cardiac rhythm showing no acute issues.
Patient has abuse history involving opioids, sedatives, and hypnotics. No known allergies reported from scene sources.
SpO2 showed fluctuation but stabilized with supplemental oxygen after initial rise. His initial NPA was removed due to agitation but replaced if needed on arrival.
He is suspected of ingesting unknown substance, possibly related to drug abuse history. Scene was searched but no substances identified for testing.
